questions.question: What is the relationship between pitch and power in straight-and-level flight?
questions.correct_answer: Pitch controls airspeed and power controls altitude (in the long term, they are interrelated)
distractors: Pitch always controls altitude and power always controls airspeed
Pitch and power have no relationship to each other
Power controls airspeed only during climbs
questions.explanation: In practice, pitch and power are interrelated. A common technique: pitch for airspeed, power for altitude in stabilized flight. Changing one typically requires adjusting the other.

questions.question: What is the difference between best rate of climb speed (VY) and best angle of climb speed (VX)?
questions.correct_answer: VY gains the most altitude per unit of time; VX gains the most altitude per unit of distance
distractors: VY gains the most altitude per unit of distance; VX per unit of time
VY and VX are the same speed at sea level
VY is always faster than cruise speed
questions.explanation: VY = best rate (most feet per minute). VX = best angle (most feet per mile of ground distance). VX is used for obstacle clearance; VY for efficient climb to altitude.

questions.question: During a power-off glide, what determines the glide distance of an aircraft?
questions.correct_answer: The lift-to-drag ratio (L/D) and wind conditions
distractors: The weight of the aircraft only
The engine RPM at idle
The altitude above the ground only
questions.explanation: Maximum glide distance is achieved at the best L/D speed. Weight affects the speed at which best L/D occurs but not the glide ratio itself. Wind will affect ground distance covered.

questions.question: What causes an aircraft to turn?
questions.correct_answer: The horizontal component of lift when the aircraft is banked
distractors: Rudder input that yaws the nose
Differential thrust between left and right engines
Ailerons directly turning the aircraft
questions.explanation: In a banked turn, lift is tilted. The horizontal component of lift pulls the aircraft toward the center of the turn. Ailerons bank the wings; the bank creates the turning force.

questions.question: What is the purpose of trim?
questions.correct_answer: To relieve control pressures so the pilot does not have to hold constant force on the controls
distractors: To change the aircraft's airspeed
To adjust the wing's angle of incidence in flight
To lock the flight controls in position
questions.explanation: Trim adjusts the aerodynamic force on the control surface (via a trim tab or other mechanism) to eliminate the need for the pilot to hold continuous pressure.

questions.question: What is the purpose of practicing flight at minimum controllable airspeed (slow flight)?
questions.correct_answer: To develop the pilot's feel for the aircraft's controls at high angles of attack near stall
distractors: To practice energy conservation for fuel savings
To demonstrate the aircraft can fly below VFE
To prepare for aerobatic maneuvers
questions.explanation: Slow flight teaches pilots to recognize and control the aircraft at high AoA, where controls are mushy, induced drag is high, and the aircraft is near stall — a critical skill for takeoff and landing phases.

questions.question: What does the ball in the inclinometer indicate?
questions.correct_answer: Whether the aircraft is in coordinated flight (slip or skid)
distractors: The rate of turn
The bank angle
The aircraft's pitch attitude
questions.explanation: The ball (slip/skid indicator) shows coordination. Ball centered = coordinated. Ball to the inside of turn = slipping. Ball to the outside = skidding. Step on the ball to correct.
